A tribute to Albert Hofmann on his 100th birthday: The mysterious discovery of LSD and the impact of psychedelics on parapsychology
===================================================================================================================================

* January 2006

THE PARANORMAL REVIEW

A TRIBUTE TO ALBERT HOFMANN ON HIS 100TH

BIRTHDAY: THE MYSTERIOUS DISCOVERY OF LSD AND THE

IMPACT OF PSYCHEDELICS ON PARAPSYCHOLOGY

DAVID LUKE

A LITTLE OVER SIXTY YEARS AGO the

Swiss   chemist   Dr   Albert  Hofmann,

following a ‘peculiar presentiment’, broke

strict  laboratory  protocol and  went  back

and investigated an apparently medically-

ineffective chemical he had created from a

number  of   similar  substances  some   five

years   earlier.   Perhaps   somehow

accidentally   ingesting   some   of   the

chemical,   Dr   Hofmann   began

experiencing quite unusual sensations and

perceptions and went home on his bicycle.

Upon  reaching  home   he   experienced  “a

nice   dreamlike  state  which  then  passed

away”  (Grof,  2004,  p.   285).  This   led   to

the   discovery   of   lysergic   acid

diethylamide (LSD), and marked probably

the most significant event in research into

consciousness and its altered states.

Born  on  11th  January,  1906,  Albert

Hofmann  celebrated  his  one  hundredth

birthday this month and a few days later

delivered   a   paper   at   a   three-day

conference   on   LSD   celebrating   his

centenary in his home   town  of  Basel,

Switzerland,  where he  once  worked  at

Sandoz   Pharmaceuticals.   Following

Hofmann’s  discovery  in 1943,  Sandoz

manufactured this potent  new   substance,

which is psychoactive even  at a mere 50

micrograms   (50  millionths   of   a   gram!).

Keen  to  see  this  wonder  drug  usefully

applied, Sandoz distributed it freely to registered

researchers  around   the globe  throughout  the

1950’s. Initially  it had  an enormous  impact  on

psychological  science,  particularly  psychiatry

and   psychotherapy.   The   Czechoslovakian

psychiatrist  Stanislav  Grof,  one  of the co-

originators   of   transpersonal   psychology,

conducted more than four thousand psychedelic

therapy  sessions  over  a  two  decade  period,

beginning with  LSD  in  1956.  Grof  (2001)

described how out-of-body experiences (OBEs)

and accurate remote viewing were observed on

a daily basis, and he has suggested that

psychedelics  such  as   LSD  could   do  for

psychiatry  what  the  microscope   has  done  for

biology  and   medicine. LSD’s application  as   a

tool  for creativity,  inspiration  and problem

solving also quickly became legendary (Stafford

&  Golightly,  1967)  and  it  is  reported  that

Francis  Crick, the discoverer  of the  DNA

double-helix, is one of a number of Nobel-prize

winning  scientists   who   have   made   their

breakthroughs  with  the  use  of  LSD  (MAPS,

2005). By  the 1960’s LSD had begun filtering

out  of  the  research  environments  and   therapy

rooms  and   subsequently  made   a   hugely

January 2006 7

THE PARANORMAL REVIEW

significant   cultural  impact,  both   negative   and

positive,   creating   an explosion  in  art,  music,

literature,   language,   and   fashion.  It   also

produced  a   rising   tide   of   psychological

exploration  that  buoyed  the  New  Age and

Human  Potential  social   movements,   sweeping

these and all manner of alternative concepts of

reality and  mystical doctrines out  of  the

backwaters  and   into  the   mainstream  (Stevens,

1988).

An entire generation of people became open

to  the   possibility  of  untapped psychic powers

and,  ensuing   from   common   experiences,   the

psychedelic  counter-culture  lifestyle  simply

promulgated  the   paranormal  as  something

actually quite normal (Krippner & Fersch,

1970). Such pro-paranormal attitudes stimulated

a   keen   interest  in   parapsychology,   and other

fringe sciences and esoteric branches of religion

also blossomed at this time. California became a

hub   for  boundary research  and  subsequently

transpersonal psychology and ‘Silicon Valley’s’

computer industry launched  themselves off  the

crest of   this  wave  of  expanded thought.

However, the impact of LSD on parapsychology

was much  more immediate  and direct than the

later surge in alternative culture,  and direct psi

research had   actually  begun   in  earnest even

before the  movement of the 1960’s, built on a

plethora  of  anecdotal  reports  of  paranormal

experiences  amongst   the  earliest  psychedelic

researchers (Stevens, 1988).

The earliest and perhaps the most fascinating

story is that  of Hofmann’s actual  discovery of

LSD,  which he first   created  in  1938 whilst

routinely   working   his   way   through   the

development   of   a   number   of   chemical

derivatives from  ergot,  a   fungi found growing

on rye. The new substance, LSD-25, the twenty-

fifth in that particular series of ergot derivatives,

was sent to the pharmacological labs to be tested

for  potential medical properties but the results

were  negative and,  as was  the  protocol,  the

chemical  was  shelved  along  with  so  many

others.

Hofmann (1983) writes: “And yet I could not

forget  the  relatively  uninteresting  LSD-25.  A

peculiar presentiment  – the feeling  that  this

substance could  possess  properties other  than

those  established  in  the  first  investigations  –

induced me, five years after the first synthesis,

to produce LSD-25 once again so that a sample

could   be   given   to   the   pharmacological

department  for  further  tests. This was  quite

unusual;  experimental  substances,  as a  rule,

were definitely stricken from the research

program if once  found to be  lacking in

pharmacological interest”.

What  followed  was that,   on  Friday,  April

16th 1943, Albert conducted the re-synthesis of

LSD-25 and having completed it by midday he

suddenly   began   to   feel   “a   remarkable

restlessness,  combined  with a slight  dizziness”

(Hofmann, 1983). He opted to cycle home, and

upon   arriving   there   he   “perceived   an

uninterrupted  stream   of   fantastic   pictures,

extraordinary shapes with intense kaleidoscopic

play  of   colours.  After some  two  hours   this

condition faded away”. Completely bemused by

the  experience,   Albert  initially  suspected  that

the solvent used in the process  had caused the

reaction, but on investigation this turned out not

to  be   the case  (Grof, 2004). He then  reasoned

that he must have ingested some of the freshly

made chemical. He returned to the laboratory on

Monday, April 19th with the purpose of testing

the  new   chemical  on   himself,  cautiously

intending to administer a tiny amount, and then

titrate the dosage upwards. Albert measured out

a mere 0.25 milligrams, an amount far less then

the most active  known  drug at  that  time.

However,  this   turned out   to  be  a   very strong

dosage for LSD, and Albert soon found himself

peddling home on his bicycle once again feeling

quite out of the ordinary, yet with a new sense

of  timelessness.   “I   also   had   the   sensation   of

being   unable   to   move   from   the   spot.

Nevertheless, my assistant later told me that we

had travelled very rapidly” (Hofmann, 1983).

On reaching  home this second time Albert

reported that “Occasionally I felt as if I were out

of  my  body.  I thought  I   had   died. My   ego

seemed suspended  somewhere  in  space,   from

where I saw my dead body lying  on the  sofa”

(quoted  in  Asperen  De  Boer, Barkema, &

Kappers, 1966, p. 448). However,  in   time  the

fear passed  and  the OBE’s abated and Albert’s

death experience turned into one of rebirth with

a  pleasurable  visual  experience  and  marked

synaesthesia. The effects finally wore off many

hours  after  ingesting  the  drug  (Grof,  2004;

Hofmann, 1983).

So   this   is   how   Hofmann’s   ‘peculiar

presentiment’  lead to the discovery of what is

still the most potent psychoactive agent known,

changing the face of culture as we know it, and

enabling a great many people to have seemingly

paranormal experiences  of every kind (see

8 Issue Thirty-Seven

THE PARANORMAL REVIEW

Luke, in  press). Psychedelic chemist Dave

Nichols (2003) pondered the nature of Albert’s

first supposed LSD experience and wondered if

this  was  not  actually  a  spontaneous  mystical

experience  merely coinciding  with Albert’s re-

creation of LSD, to which Albert replied that it

was ‘entirely possible’.  As   a child Albert  had

had  a   number  of  mystical   nature  experiences

(Hofmann,  1983).  Nichols  suggested  that  it

would be very difficult and exceedingly unlikely

for  a  meticulous  chemist,  such  as  Albert,  to

actually accidentally ingest the necessary 50-75

micrograms that would be required to stimulate

such an experience as he had that first time on

the   Friday.  Furthermore,  Nichols  has   pointed

out that two-hour-long LSD trips are unheard of

and that LSD very reliably induces trips of eight

hours  or  more,  much  like   Albert’s later

controlled dosage  experiment  on  the   Monday.

Nichols further found it inexplicable that Albert

should have  had a peculiar presentiment  to   go

back  five years later  and, against protocol and

logic,  specifically  investigate  just  the  twenty-

fifth in a whole series of rejected compounds.

Occultist Julian Vayne (2001, p82) notes that

“the  magical  traditions  and  the   shamanic

complex recognize the  drug as having its  own

‘spirit’, and asks whether ‘the “spirit” of LSD’

spoke to  Hofmann?”, thereby causing his

presentiment.   This   suggestion   has   some

resonance with the findings of  a recent  survey

into  the  occurrence of paranormal experiences

with  psychoactive  substances,   in  which  some

SPR lecture attendees took part (Luke &

Kittenis,   2006).   Of   the   sample,   three

respondents reported sensing the  LSD spirit or

intelligence   –   an   experience  which  was  very

much   more   commonly   reported   with

psychedelic plants like ‘magic’ mushrooms but

curiously   absent   with   purely   synthetic

psychedelic  substances  such  as ketamine  or

MDMA.  Perhaps  this   is  so because LSD is  a

semi-synthetic/semi organic compound, thereby

revealing  a  graded  relationship  between  the

notion of sentience and the idea of life.

However, whether LSD actually has sentience is

unlikely to be empirically tested or proven, but

its ingestion  can  certainly  bestow  such an

experience.

That Albert Hofmann had  his ‘peculiar

presentiment’, as well as an OBE and a death-

rebirth experience with LSD,  no  doubt piqued

his,  and   others’,  interest   in  parapsychological

research with psychedelics. By 1949, LSD had

become  available  to   researchers in  the   United

States  and elsewhere,  but   it  took   a  few  years

more before they actually began publishing their

findings   of   its   use,   initially   as   a

‘psychotomimetic’  drug used  in  modelling

psychosis  and later  as a  therapeutic  agent.

Nevertheless,   even   in   those   earliest

experimental  years  LSD was also investigated

for its parapsychological potential, following on

from the seminal investigations with mescaline

in  the  early  1950’s by the English physicians,

Humphrey   Osmond   and   John   Smythies,

(Smythies, 1983, 1987). Osmond later gave the

writer  Aldous  Huxley  mescaline in  1953,  and

Osmond  coined  the   phrase   ‘psychedelic’,

meaning ‘mind-manifesting’, in correspondence

with  Huxley.   Following  which,   Huxley  then

popularised   the   use   of   LSD   and   other

psychedelics  among  the  cognoscenti   until   his

death in 1963. It wasn’t until the early to mid

1960’s  that psychedelics   became   popularised

among the masses by figures such as the rogue

psychologist Timothy Leary, who began a

psilocybin project  at Harvard after  first  trying

the substance himself in 1960 (Stevens, 1988).

Yet, even before the 1960’s, when the  term

psychedelic came into the popular vernacular of

the   wider   scientific   community,   the

Parapsychology   Foundation   had   already

organised two international conferences on

parapsychology  and psychedelics.  The   first  of

which took place in New York in 1958 and the

second in France in 1959, the same year as the

first  international  conference  on  psychedelic

psychotherapy. In attendance in both years, the

famous  medium  Eileen  Garrett  (1961,  p.  61)

reported that:  “My   experience  with   LSD   has

been   that   it   enhanced   the   mediumistic

experience as  such, but not  the guessing

technique with cards and symbols which are not

related  in   my   mind  to  mediumship”;   adding

that:  “I   have  had  psychic  experiences  which

occur at   the  height  of   the  LSD   experience.  I

believe  the drug  has   made  me   a   better,  more

accurate sensitive when  I   perceive,  hear, think

and feel” (p. 63). By this point some amount of

informal research  had been conducted  and

several  structured  research  programs followed

on  from these conferences. By  1966 there had

been eight in-depth published studies conducted

with   psychedelics,   primarily   LSD   and

psilocybin, which  reported varying degrees  of

success in eliciting ESP (for a review see Luke,

in press). For at least one of which (Cavanna &

January 2006 7

THE PARANORMAL REVIEW

Servadio, 1964)  Albert  Hofmann personally

provided   technical   advice,   regarding

theappropriate substances to use.

The  Parapsychology Foundation ran a third

conference  in  New York  later in  1967, just  as

LSD became illegal in the United States. Albert

Hoffman  was  in   attendance  and  offered  his

expertise  on psychopharmacology  at various

points,  even  delivering an   anecdote  about the

amateur  mycologist  Gordon   Wasson  (Levine,

1968).   Wasson   had   hypothesised   that

mushrooms   had   once   been   used   as   a

sacramental  in  religious  rites  in  the  ancient

world and finally found  living evidence of this

on an expedition to an obscure region of Mexico

in 1953, following up a  possible lead from the

mythologist  and  poet  Robert   Graves.  Wasson

observed  a  divinatory  mushroom ceremony  in

which the ‘bemushroomed’ indigenous shaman,

to his surprise, told him two things about his son

back home   that even   Wasson   could not   have

known,  which later turned out to  be true

(Wasson & Wasson, 1957).

Wasson identified  the  mushrooms  and  had

them sent to some big pharmaceutical company

chemists to  isolate the  active alkaloids,  but no

one was   able  to  identify  the   active chemicals

using   the  standard  procedures  with  animals.

Later, in   1957, Hofmann was  consulted  and

seeing  that   the mushrooms  had   no  observable

effects on animals he took some himself.

Despite  knowing   that   the  mushrooms where

Mexican,   Hofmann   (1983)   described   his

astonishment   at   everywhere   seeing   Aztec

images. “Whether my eyes were closed or open,

I saw only Mexican motifs and colors. When the

doctor supervising the experiment bent over me

to check my blood pressure, he was transformed

into an Aztec priest and I would not have been

astonished if he had drawn an obsidian knife.”

Yet this unusual experience was not uncommon,

and  Hofmann (1983) later reported that others

eating the  mushrooms would often  naïvely  see

ancient Mexican art images, perhaps like a kind

of  spontaneous  psychometry  of   the  traditional

users  of  the   mushroom,  although alternatively

Wasson  proposed that  these  same  visions had

actually influenced the original ancient-Mexican

art forms.

By 1958, using himself and his assistants as

test  subjects Hofmann had managed  to   isolate

the two psychoactive principles, which he called

‘psilocybin’  and   ‘psilocin’  after the Psilocybe

genus  of mushroom from  which they  came.

This, seemingly, is merely coincidental with the

term  ‘psi’,  introduced  a  few  years  earlier  by

Thouless (1942). After  developing  a  technique

for synthesising these alkaloids, Hoffman  went

with Wasson on a return  journey to Mexico in

1962  and   gave the Mazatec  shamaness,  Maria

Sabina,  his  psilocybin  pills  in   place  of   actual

mushrooms  to  use  during  a  ceremony.  The

shamaness happily conceded that the pills

contained the  mushroom ‘spirit’ that gives the

divinatory information, and she was able to use

them  accurately  to  tell   Wasson  of  his   distant

daughter’s   well-being,   although   Hofmann

(1983)  notes   that   this   occasion  was  no  great

confirmation of real psychic ability.

Hofmann  then   went   on   to   study  the

chemistry  of  ‘ololiuqui’,  the   seeds  of   another

Mexican  divinatory  plant,  a  species  of  the

flowering  climber  ‘morning  glory’,  which

Wasson had supplied him with.  Hofmann was

successful in  isolating the alkaloid  of this too

and was astonished to find that it was a lysergic

acid amide chemically very  similar  to the

unique  LSD  molecule  that  he   had  some   years

earlier discovered. “I realized that LSD is really

just a small chemical modification of a very old

sacred  drug of Mexico”  (Hofmann,  cited  in

Grof,  2004, p.  289).  Albert   termed  this  his

‘magic   circle’   because   it   completed   his

psychedelic  research  from  the  lysergic  acid

amides  of   ergot, which  lead   onto  mushrooms

and then back to lysergic acid amides again with

morning glory, a completely foreign plant

family  to   ergot,  a lower  fungus.  That   Albert

should  also discover  such  a  close, naturally

occurring, chemical analogue to LSD elsewhere

in  the  plant  kingdom  was   met  initially  with

incredulity among  his colleagues,  who thought

it so unlikely that  they initially accused him of

contaminating his samples. This ‘dirty test tube’

accusation, familiar to all researchers who make

outlandish discoveries, was of course disproved

with subsequent reanalysis.

In later years, Albert collaborated with

Gordon  Wasson  and   Carl   Ruck  in developing

and presenting the theory that kykeon, the secret

sacramental  used  during  initiations  into  the

Mysteries  of  Eleusis  by  the  ancient  Greeks

between  1400  BC   and   400   CE,  was  in fact a

psychedelic  potion   derived  from  ergot.   This

extraordinary   thesis,   well   supported   and

researched, was based on the importance in the

Mysteries of Demeter as a goddess of grain and

ergot, and was published as the Road to Eleusis

8 Issue Thirty-Seven

THE PARANORMAL REVIEW

(Wasson, Hofmann, & Ruck, 1978). Albert also

collaborated with  the  esteemed director  of   the

Harvard   Botanical   Museum,   Dr   Richard

Schultes, in producing the beautifully illustrated

and extensively researched  masterpiece  Plants

of  the  Gods, which  identifies  the ethnobotany,

chemistry, anthropology and the traditional

ritual  use  of  nearly 100  different genera  of

sacred  and   divinatory  plants   across  the  world

(Schultes & Hofmann, 1980, 1992). This great

work  is  an  invaluable  resource  for  anyone

hoping to learn more of the diversity of

substances that have traditionally been used for

paranormal purposes, and this work alone

qualifies  Albert  as a  great  contributor   to   the

field   aside   from   his   enormous   indirect

contributions through his chemical discoveries.

In LSD, my problem child, Albert Hofmann

(1983) wrote that:  “in the LSD state the

boundaries  between  the  experiencing  self   and

the  outer   world  more  or  less  disappear. …

Feedback between  receiver  and sender  takes

place. A portion of the self  overflows  into the

outer world, into objects, which begin to live, to

have  another,   a  deeper   meaning.   In   an

auspicious  case,  the  new  ego  feels  blissfully

united with  the  objects  of the outer world  and

consequently  also  with  its  fellow  beings. This

experience   of  deep  oneness  with   the   exterior

world can even intensify to a feeling of the self

being one with the universe.”

At various points  the   presidential  addresses

given to both the Society for Psychical Research

(Donald  West,  1965)  and   the  Parapsychology

Association (Ian  Stevenson, 1981) have  noted

the importance of LSD and related substances in

the continued investigation of ESP. The study of

parapsycho-pharmacology has so far been fairly

limited  but,  nevertheless,  rather  fruitful  and

continues to offer valuable insights to the field

(see  Luke,  in   press). In  looking to understand

the relationship between mind and matter,

subject and object, individual and universe, the

discoveries   and   contributions   of   Albert

Hofmann have made a very significant impact.

Happy 100th birthday Albert!

Note: at the time of writing, Hofmann’s LSD, my

problem child has long been out of print so I have

been  working  from   an  electronic  version,

available   online   at

http://nepenthes.lycaeum.org

/drugs/LSD/probchild/, which  unfortunately has

no page numbers for the quotes. A new edition,

published by MAPS, is due out presently.

References

with  LSD  25 and psilocybin. A  methodological

to mediumship. In Anon. (Ed.), Proceedings on Two

(pp.  14-16,  & 61-63).   New  York:   Parapsychology

Foundation.

Anomalous Psychedelic Experiences: At the Neurochemical Juncture of the Humanistic and Parapsychological

Article

Full-text available

* May 2020

* ![David LukeDavid Luke](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Luke-2)

This article explores the nature of psychedelically induced anomalous experiences for what they reveal regarding the nature of “expanded consciousness” and its implications for humanistic and transpersonal psychology, parapsychology, and the psychology and underlying neuroscience of such experiences. Taking a multidisciplinary approach, this essay reviews the nature of 10 transpersonal or parapsychological experiences that commonly occur spontaneously and in relation to the use of psychedelic substances, namely synesthesia, extradimensional percepts, out-of-body experiences, near-death experiences, entity encounters, alien abduction, sleep paralysis, interspecies communication, possession, and psi (telepathy, precognition, and clairvoyance and psychokinesis).

By the middle of the 20 th century the Swiss chemist Albert Hofmann had discovered LSD-experiencing its OBE-inducing propertiesand isolated, named, and synthesized psilocybin and psilocin, the psychoactive derivatives of the visionary Psilocybe mushroom genus (Hofmann, 1983;Luke, 2006). Psychoactive Substances and Paranormal Phenomena: A Comprehensive Review

Article

Full-text available

* Jan 2012

* ![David LukeDavid Luke](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Luke-2)

This paper investigates the relationship between psychoactive substances and so-called paranormal
phenomena falling within the study of parapsychology. It is primarily concerned with extrasensory
perception (ESP)—telepathy, precognition, and clairvoyance—as well as out-of-body experiences
(OBEs) and near-death experiences (NDEs). Psychokinesis (PK), aura vision, encounter experiences,
and sleep paralysis only make a very limited contribution to this review as they are seldom related
to psychoactive drugs within the parapsychological literature. The paper borrows widely, but by no
means exhaustively, from parapsychology as well as transpersonal studies, anthropology, ethnobotany,
phytochemistry, psychiatry, psychotherapy, psychopharmacology, and neurobiology, particularly
neurochemistry. It is organized into neurochemical models of paranormal experience (section 1),
field reports of intentional and spontaneous phenomena incorporating anthropological, historical
and clinical cases, and personal accounts (section 2), surveys of paranormal belief and experience
(section 3), experimental research (section 4), and a methodological critique of the experimental
research with recommendations for further work (section 5).

Anthropology and Parapsychology: Still Hostile Sisters in Science?

Article

Full-text available

* Nov 2010

* ![David LukeDavid Luke](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Luke-2)

After a long, slow journey from the leather armchairs of its forefathers to out-of-body travels over Amazonian jungles, the anthropology of psi has progressed through a number of historical, methodological, and ontological developments. The course of this transformation from the detached and disbelieving dismissal of the occult to the engaged and emic entertainment of psi as a scientific possibility is discussed. Following a century of obscurity within anthropology, the notion of magic as psi finally found unique refuge within the anthropology of consciousness. Nevertheless, despite decades of research anthropological parapsychology can still be considered a completely nascent field of study and is speculated to remain so until its interdisciplinary imperative is actually fulfilled and its subject matter is shared fully by the disciplines that border it.

Microdosing psychedelics: More questions than answers? An overview and suggestions for future research

Article

Full-text available

* Jul 2019
* J PSYCHOPHARMACOL

* ![Kim P. C. KuypersKim P. C. Kuypers](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Kim-Kuypers)
* ![Livia NgLivia Ng](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Livia-Ng-2)
* ![David ErritzoeDavid Erritzoe](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Erritzoe)
* ![David J NuttDavid J Nutt](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Nutt-2)

Background
In the past few years, the issue of ‘microdosing’ psychedelics has been openly discussed in the public arena where claims have been made about their positive effect on mood state and cognitive processes such as concentration. However, there are very few scientific studies that have specifically addressed this issue, and there is no agreed scientific consensus on what microdosing is.
Aim
This critique paper is designed to address questions that need to be answered by future scientific studies and to offer guidelines for these studies.
Approach
Owing to its proximity for a possible approval in clinical use and short-lasting pharmacokinetics, our focus is predominantly on psilocybin. Psilocybin is allegedly, next to lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD), one of the two most frequently used psychedelics to microdose. Where relevant and available, data for other psychedelic drugs are also mentioned.
Conclusion
It is concluded that while most anecdotal reports focus on the positive experiences with microdosing, future research should also focus on potential risks of (multiple) administrations of a psychedelic in low doses. To that end, (pre)clinical studies including biological (e.g. heart rate, receptor turnover and occupancy) as well as cognitive (e.g. memory, attention) parameters have to be conducted and will shed light on the potential negative consequences microdosing could have.

A preliminary survey of paranormal experiences with psychoactive drugs

Article

Full-text available

* Sep 2005

* ![David LukeDavid Luke](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Luke-2)
* ![Marios KittenisMarios Kittenis](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Marios-Kittenis-2)

The occurrence of transpersonal experiences with psychedelic substances is well attested, and several surveys have consistently found a small relationship between paranormal experiences and the use of such drugs in general. Isolated investigations of experiences with specific drugs exist, but no surveys have systematically examined whether particular experiences relate to particular drugs. In an online survey, 139 respondents were recruited through parapsychology or psychedelic interest groups and completed a questionnaire detailing psychoactive drug-use behaviour and the frequency of occurrence of a number of paranormal, shamanic, and mystical type experiences. Patterns of drug-induced transpersonal experiences reported elsewhere were mostly corroborated, particularly the proclivity for telepathic experiences with cannabis, out-of-body experiences with ketamine, entity encounter experiences with N,N-dimethyltryptamine (DMT), and plant-spirit encounters with a host of psychedelic plants. Several small correlations were found between drug-use frequency and experience frequency with certain drug and experience types, particularly those termed mystical. As expected, alcohol and opiate/opioid-use did not correlate with any transpersonal experiences although, surprisingly, no sizable correlations were found for psi experiences and the use of any one type of drug, possibly due to the high rate of occurrence of psi experiences among both drug users and non-drug users with this particular sample.

### Albert Hofmann, LSD and the formation of the lost field of parapsychopharmacology

April 2009

* !David Luke[David Luke](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Luke-2)

In 1943, following a peculiar presentiment to re-synthesize LSD-25, Dr. Albert Hofmann accidentally had his first psychedelic experience with this chemical and consequently had his first out-of-body experience as well. It has been well documented that, beginning in the early 1950s, psychological and psychophysiological research with LSD and other psychedelics boomed until prohibition in the late ... [Show full abstract] 1960s, but little has been reported about the significant influence of psychedelics on, and Hofmann’s contribution to parapsychological research at that time. This paper draws attention to the importance attached to paranormal phenomena among the earliest and most famous psychedelic discoverers and researchers in the developed world, along with the subsequent interest in psychedelics among parapsychologists, thereby plotting the course of the formation of what might be called the lost field of parapsychopharmacology. It is also asked that, given that we are now witnessing a renaissance in psychedelic research, isn’t it time that anthropologists, parapsychologists and psychedelic researchers again unite to once more investigate the profound paranormal experiences people more frequently report with these substances, to find out what they can genuinely add to our potential as human beings.
More Info: Luke, D. P. (2009, April). Albert Hofmann, LSD and the formation of the lost field of parapsychopharmacology. Paper presented at the 29th Annual Conference of the Society for the Anthropology of Consciousness, Portland, Oregon, USA.

### Becoming Modern in China with an Indigenous Amazonian Psychedelic Brew

November 2023

* [Alex K. Gearin](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Alex-K-Gearin-2119083023)

The first collection of its kind to explore the diverse and global history of psychedelics as they appealed to several generations of researchers and thinkers.
Expanding Mindscapes offers a fascinatingly fluid and diverse history of psychedelics that stretches around the globe. While much of the literature to date has focused on the history of these drugs in the United States and Canada, editors ... [Show full abstract] Erika Dyck and Chris Elcock deliberately move away from these places in this collection to reveal a longer and more global history of psychedelics, which chronicles their discovery, use, and cultural impact in the twentieth century.
The authors in this collection explore everything from LSD psychotherapy in communist Czechoslovakia to the first applications of LSD-25 in South America to the intersection of modernism and ayahuasca in China. Along the way, they also consider how psychedelic experiments generated their own cultural expressions, where the specter of the United States may have loomed large and where colonial empires exerted influence on the local reception of psychedelics in botanical and pharmaceutical pursuits.
Breaking new ground by adopting perspectives that are currently lacking in the historiography of psychedelics, this collection adds to the burgeoning field by offering important discussions on underexplored topics such as gender, agriculture, parapsychology, anarchism, and technological innovations.
